coredata.dc:title: Preparation and performance study of dye-based carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, JianFeng
Ma, XingYuan
Ma, MingLan
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of Full-color carbon dots is well established, but the explanation of its mechanism is not only highly controversial, but partly even contradictory to the project. Therefore, to investigate the red-shift mechanism of Full-color carbon dots, citric acid and Nile blue with complex composition structure were selected as raw materials, and B-,G-,Y- and R-CQDs were prepared by changing the reaction solvent with quantum yields of 49.7 %,28.2 %,72.3 %,30.3 % and average particle sizes of mainly 2.7–3.2 nm, respectively. TEM and XPS tests show that the emission wavelength redshift is mainly attributed to the reduction of the energy band gap due to the sp2 conjugate size and the increase of graphitic nitrogen content. To examine the optical properties of dye-based F-CQDs, F-CQDs/PVA polymer films and LEDs were prepared, both of which showed unique optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Conjugate size
Graphitic nitrogen

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HRTEM images and size distribution diagrams of (a)B-CQDs,(b)G-CQDs, (c)Y-CQDs, (d)R-CQDs. The inserted photos are the corresponding CQDs under 365 nm UV light.
(a) FT-IR spectra of the F-CQDs;(b) UV–vis absorption spectra of F-CQDs;(c) Normalized PL spectra;(d) Time-resolved PL spectra.
(a) High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra; (b) Possible mechanism of F-CQDs.
Full color CQDs/PVA composite CIE chart (a) B-, (b) G-, (c) Y-, (d) R-. (The top right inset shows the corresponding film, and the bottom right shows the corresponding LED).
Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs/PVA films.

head.ce:title: Preparation and performance study of dye-based carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Polymer and Carbon Matrix Composites Laboratory, Flexible Electronics Institute, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xian 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Polymer and Carbon Matrix Composites Laboratory
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Polymer and Carbon Matrix Composites Laboratory, Flexible Electronics Institute, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xian 710021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Full-color carbon dots（F-CQDs）were prepared by changing the reaction solvent using the dye Nair Blue and citric acid as raw materials.

The preparation of Full-color carbon dots is well established, but the explanation of its mechanism is not only highly controversial, but partly even contradictory to the project. Therefore, to investigate the red-shift mechanism of Full-color carbon dots, citric acid and Nile blue with complex composition structure were selected as raw materials, and B-,G-,Y- and R-CQDs were prepared by changing the reaction solvent with quantum yields of 49.7 %,28.2 %,72.3 %,30.3 % and average particle sizes of mainly 2.7–3.2 nm, respectively. TEM and XPS tests show that the emission wavelength redshift is mainly attributed to the reduction of the energy band gap due to the sp2 conjugate size and the increase of graphitic nitrogen content. To examine the optical properties of dye-based F-CQDs, F-CQDs/PVA polymer films and LEDs were prepared, both of which showed unique optical properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor photoluminescent materials have a vast field of applications (e.g., nanosensor, bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, fluorescent nanoprobe, anti-counterfeiting, and information encryption), they have received significant attention in recent years [1-5]. Common Full-color photoluminescent materials mainly include rare earth, chalcogenides, semiconductor quantum dots, etc [6-8]. However, these traditional materials are generally difficult to synthesize, poorly water-soluble, poor bio-solubility, and contain toxicity, all of which severely limit their wide application [9-12]. Therefore, it is of great value and significance to prepare new Full-color photoluminescent materials with good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and non-toxicity by a simple and safe method.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), emerging nanomaterials that produce a variety of unique properties due to electron leaps, can also be called a library of emerging carbon materials that are generally smaller than 10 nm in size [13]. CQDs not only have multicolor emission properties, but also have excellent water solubility and biocompatibility, and are widely source, simple to prepare, and non-toxic [13,14]. The photoluminescence properties of Full carbon quantum dots(F-CQDs) are mainly determined by the structure and/or surface state, but they will depend on distinct synthesis methods or precursors. The hydrothermal/thermal solvent method is one of the most popular methods for preparing F-CQDs. In former studies, F-CQDs were prepared by changing the reaction solvent to control the surface state and/or sp2 size [8,15-17]; The degree of graphitization, surface functionalization or surface oxidation was controlled by varying the mass ratio of the two precursors in the hot solvent reaction to prepare F-CQDs, and the Quantum Yield (QY) of F-CQDs increased with increasing graphitic N content (up to 74 %) [9,18]; By modifying the pH of the deionized water in the hydrothermal reaction to control the large size of the sp2 conjugated structural domain and increasing the amount of graphitic nitrogen, the band gap of CDs is narrowed, leading to a shift of PL red to the lower energy end of the visible light [19,20]; Typically, F-CQDs (blue, green and red) were prepared from three isomers of phenylenediamine with controlled nanoparticle size and N content by a hot solvent method [21]; Besides the traditional methods mentioned above, there are new developments in the preparation of F-CQDs, such as, recyclable method [22]. In addition, the separation of F-CQDs from prepared samples by gel permeation chromatography is also a good method, but its biggest drawback is the waste of a large amount of solvent and a long preparation cycle with low yields [23,24]. To date, the preparation of F-CQDs has been very mature, and the application range is quite wide, but the explanation of the red-shift mechanism of F-CQDs is not only controversial but also conflicting, which is mainly due to the wide range of raw materials and preparation methods of F-CQDs.

As the research progresses, the traditional raw materials are no more satisfied to explore the relationship between the structure and properties of F-CQDs, hence, in Recent years, dyes have gradually come into view as raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs. Zheng and Gao's team used dyes as raw materials to prepare red discs for biomedical imaging and metal ion detection. Strongly absorbing and near-infrared (NIR) emitting CQDs have also been prepared using hydrophobic cyanide dyes, which have excellent NIR fluorescence imaging and are ideal therapeutic agents for in vitro photothermal therapy [25-27]. Most of the dyes are rich in molecular structure, contain large conjugate structures, and contain nitrogen and other characteristic elements, which help to increase the conjugate size and the content of doped nitrogen elements in CQDs, make the emission wavelength red-shift and improve the QY of CQDs, which are the best raw materials for preparing F-CQDs and exploring the structure and properties of CQDs.

However, almost no F-CQDs have been prepared using dyes as raw materials. Herein, we report a rational synthesis of blue, green, yellow, red CQDs (The abbreviation is B-,G-,Y-,R-CQDs only by changing the reaction solvent(Water,2.8 mol/L dilute sulfuric acid solution and ethanol,water and DMF,Water and ethanol) using Nair blue and citric acid as raw materials. The PLQY of the different CQDs with B-,G-,Y- and R- emissions reached 49.7 %,28.2 %,72.3 %,30.3 %. TEM and XPS examinations show that the F-CQDs obtained from dye preparations result from an increase in conjugate size and graphitic nitrogen content. In addition, the prepared F-CQDs were applied to light-emitting diodes (LED) and fluorescent F-CQDs/PVA composite films.

The morphology and Structure characterizations of the F-CQDs including TEM,FT-IR. TEM and HRTEM imaging are shown in Fig. 1 . TEM images reveal that the F-CQDs are spherical with average particle sizes of 2.7,3.0,3.1,3.2 nm for B-,G-,Y-,R-CQDs, respectively. The HRTEM of F-CQDs exhibits very well-defined lattice stripes, which are mainly ascribed to the (100) lattice planes of graphite, suggesting that all CQDs have a similar structure to graphite [8,17,28]. The characteristic functional groups on the surface of F-CQDs were analyzed by FR-TR spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2 a. The significant broad peaks in the 3400–3600 cm−1 range are ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of the hydroxyl(–OH) and amino(–NH2) groups. The two weaker peaks at 2973 cm−1 and 2894 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of –CH3 and –CH2. This is consistent with the results of other work [29-31].Another band at 1630 cm−1 corresponds to the stretch vibration of CC/CO.1060–1200 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CO. This is proof that all four CQDs contain functional groups of oxygen and nitrogen on their surfaces.

Characterization of the optical properties of F-CQDs using UV–vis and PL spectra. The UV absorption spectra of the F-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2b. All samples had significant absorption peaks in the range of 270–280 nm, which was mainly assigned to the π-π* transition of C = C bonds. In addition, the peak located at ∼ 350 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO [32]. However, in the lower-energy region, Y- and R-CQDs display distinct absorption bands at 450–520 nm and 500–600 nm, respectively, suggesting that these samples possess different surface states[16,17], otherwise due to the n-π* transition in the CO bond[33,34]. The maximum fluorescence absorption peaks of the four CQDs are positioned at 460 nm, 495 nm, 570 nm, and 635 nm respectively, covering the whole visible range (Fig. 2c). Among the four CQDs, only the B-CQDs show excitation dependence, while the other three CQDs exhibit excitation independence, which is mainly attributed to the influence of the conjugation effect of the surface functional groups and carbon core, while implying a change in the sp2 conjugation size of the F-CQDs (Fig. S2 in Supporting information). Therefore, it can be concluded that the emission of CQDs is related to the size of the sp2 conjugated domain, which can be adjusted by the solvent under solvothermal conditions [16,35]. This is also in agreement with the increasing trend of carbon dot size. The PLQY of the B-, G-, Y-, and R-CQDs reached 49.7 %,28.2 %,72.3 %,30.3 %, respectively. It is well known that the key to enhancing PLQY is to promote radiative and suppress non-radiative transition by controlling the surface state electron transition process [36,37]. The electron-donating species in the carbon-dot structure raise PLQY, while the electron-withdrawing species do the opposite [38,39]. Thus, the high QY of the Y-CQDs may be due to the inhibition of nonradiative transition by the electron-donating amino group in DMF, while the low QY of the G-CQDs and R-CQDs are due to the inhibition of radiative transition by the hydroxyl group in ethanol. Summarizing the QYs of some representative F-CQDs, it can be clearly seen that the results of our work are relatively outstanding. (Table. S3 in Supporting Information).

The time-resolved PL decay curves of all samples are shown in Fig. 2d. The results (Table S1 in Supporting Information) show that they can be fitting by a double exponential formula which involves a short-term lifetime component τ1 (about 1 ns) and a long-term lifetime component τ2 (about 10 ns), which are allocated to the recombination processes in the core and surface states, respectively[40,41]. It is worth noting that from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, the average lifetime increases from 9.87 ns to 25.15 ns and then decreases to 3.15 ns. The proportion of τ1 increases from 14.0 % to 22.8 % and the proportion of τ2 decreases from 8.6 % to 0.8 %, indicating that the red shift of PL leads to an increasing effect of the core state on the radiative lifetime in these carbon sites[42,43]. The above outcomes indicate that the optical properties of our prepared F-CQDs are remarkable and their tunable photoluminescence properties can be attributed to the increase in sp2 conjugate size, which can be controlled by different reaction solvents.

To further explore the photoluminescence mechanism of F-CQDs, X-ray photoelectroscopy (XPS) was performed on four CQDs. In former studies, the CQDs emission wavelengths were red-shifted mainly owing to nitrogen and oxygen contents, hybridization effects, and surface states. Nevertheless, in our case, the XPS test results show that the F-CQDs are composed of three elements: carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, and the oxygen and nitrogen contents show no relationship with redshift (Figure. S3 in Supporting Information). In the XPS test results, the oxygen content slightly decreased and the nitrogen content slightly increased and then decreased and was mainly present in two forms: amide nitrogen(399.5 eV) and graphite nitrogen(402 eV)(Fig. 3 a). From B-CQDs to R-CQDs, the graphite nitrogen content increased from 2.7 % to 21.04 % and the amide nitrogen content decreased from 97.3 % to 78.96 % (Table.S2 in Supporting Information). According to previous studies, the electron and hole recombination fluorescence centers are related to the extent of π-electron conjugation and the content of graphitic nitrogen[44,45]. The previous TEM results show that the PL emission wavelength of CD is dependent on the size of the particles and the degree of the sp2 conjugation domain. A greater π-electron system or a higher graphitic nitrogen amount (introducing a new intermediate gap state between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals) would lead to a narrower energy gap, which would transfer the PL emission to the red region[17,46]. Rogach et al. performed calculations using density generalized function theory and came to conclusions that are consistent with the experiment [47,48]. Based on the above assay results and discussion, we conclude that the F-CQDs we prepared are caused by greater sp2 conjugate size and increased graphitic nitrogen content (Fig. 3b). In particular, the QY increased with the increase of graphite nitrogen content, the most significant one was up to 72.3 % for Y-CQDs, while the low yield of G- and R-CQDs could be due to the high acidity in the reaction vessel due to the presence of sulfuric acid and low boiling point ethanol in the reaction solvent, or the excessive pressure made the carbon dots over-carbonized and destroyed their structure[17,49] .

F-CQDs are reported to be able to be applied to polymer materials to prepare fluorescent composite films, as well as to light-emitting diodes. To compare the similarities and differences in the dispersion and binding properties of dye-based F-CQDs and other F-CQDs in polymer materials, F-CQDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) fluorescent polymer films were prepared (Fig. 4 a-d). The results show that the prepared polymer film emits bright blue,green,yellow,red colors, covering the whole visible range, and no burst, light shift, or other phenomena occur. The fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs/PVA films are shown in Fig. 5 , indicating that the PVA material does not have any effect on the fluorescence performance of CQDs. In addition, the corresponding CQDs/PVA liquid is coated on the inner wall of the lens of the 365 nm lamp bead to prepare the emitting bright blue, green, yellow, and red LED. Their CIE coordinates are (0.195,0.257), (0.231,0.412), (0.448,0.536), and (0.682,0.327), corresponding to blue, green, yellow and red, in that order. Among them, the R-CQDs/PVA polymer material is located on the horseshoe line of the CIE coordinate chart, indicating the highest red saturation.

In conclusion, we successfully prepared B-,G-,Y- and R-CQDs with QY of 49.7 %,28.2 %,72.3 %,30.3 %, respectively, by changing the reaction solvent and using dyes as the nitrogen source. TEM and XPS tests show that the emission wavelength redshift is mainly attributed to the reduction of the energy band gap due to the sp2 conjugate size and the increase of graphitic nitrogen content. To examine the optical properties of dye-based F-CQDs, F-CQDs/PVA polymer films and LEDs were prepared, both of which showed unique optical properties. This work provides a reference for investigating the luminescence mechanism of F-CQDs and provides a new concept for preparing dye-based F-CQDs.
Xing-Yuan Ma: Experimental manipulation, testing, first draft writing.
Jianfeng Li: graph drawing, analysis, revising the article.
MingLan Ma: Data Analysis Search for information Submission and Revision of Manuscripts

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding.
